summary: An employer's actual medical conditions can't be known without claims data — so this page models them. Each synthetic member is matched to real, anonymous respondents of the CDC's national health surveys who share their age, sex, race/ethnicity and income — drawn from their own state where possible — and inherits that respondent's health profile.

The result is condition rates that reflect this particular workforce — younger, hourly, retail — rather than the general population. Dollar figures appear only where published research provides a per-person annual cost for a condition; everywhere else we show how many people are affected and leave the dollars blank rather than invent them.

moves.headline: Conditions on the individual twins, not tract averages
moves.detail: Each twin gets condition flags (obesity BMI≥30 measured, diabetes diagnosed, hypertension measured) via BRFSS 2024 + NHANES 2021–23 donor matching on age × sex × race × income with a home-state geographic ring — so prevalence reflects the workforce's own demographics, fixing the ecological weakness.

moves.headline: Burden = affected covered adults × a cited cost basis
moves.detail: National prevalence × covered adults × the per-condition annual incremental cost range. Obesity/diabetes/hypertension use the MEPS/ADA ranges; depression (MEPS, Egede 2016), arthritis/MSK (MEPS, Yelin/Murphy) and smoking (literature range) are priced from published estimates. Conditions with no defensible cost basis show prevalence + headcount only — no invented dollars.

moves.headline: Real comorbidity
moves.detail: Per-twin overlap across obesity/diabetes/hypertension — impossible with ecological prevalence. Conditions may be summed within a person, never across the three.

limitations: Modeled imputation (donor hot-deck + conditional fusion), not claims — tier=modeled.
Employed-workforce burden, NOT general population (no PLACES calibration, on purpose).
Per-state prevalence only where ≥50 twins; smaller states omitted.
Worker condition rates applied to covered adults (including adult dependents).
Literature cost ranges are as published (not inflation-adjusted); the smoking range is wide because selection-adjusted estimates run well below raw per-smoker excess.
